free_text: Thermal neutron irradiation of Uranium-235, bromine,
iodine in presence of organic liquid
Primary reference.
Abstract only.
I data.
Nuclear Reactor at Phoenix Lab.
The neutron flux was 2*10**12 neutron/cm2/sec.
Uranyl Nitrate + organic Liquid.
Uranyl nitrate was irradiated either "suspended" in
n-C5H12 or dissolved in pure tertiary butyl alcohol
(henceforth TBA) or in a mixture of TBA+CH30H (3:1).
The amount of uranyl nitrate irradiated was
determined by weight. Irradiations were run using
both natural uranyl nitrate (0.715% U-235) and
enriched (90% U-235). For the former 10-30 mg uranyl
nitrate were irradiated for 3-5 min., and for the
latter 1-3 mg for 5-10 sec. The samples were
irradiated in a small (1.4 ml) polyethylene snap-cap
vial.
Gamma-ray spectra were obtained by using
either a 3"x3" NaI detector, or a Ge-Li detector.
Research has been done to determine the
independent fission yields of short-lived fission
nuclides or of fission nuclides with short-lived
precursors. By irradiating uranium in the presence of
an organic liquid the recoil fission nuclides should
react with the organic molecules to form stable
organic products. Because a fission product recoil
energy is typically about 70-100 MeV, it would be
expected to react with the organic liquid to the same
extent and result in the same organic products as
nuclides of the same element activated by the
(n,gamma) process. The recoil atoms formed by beta
decay, on the other hand, should be less reactive in
forming organic products due to the significantly
smaller recoil energy which typically would be about
1-2 eV. What is of importance is that the chemical
reaction would take place during the neutron
irradiation. Thus, the typical post-irradiation
chemical separation, and the time it would take to
perform such an operation, would be avoided
completely. However, following the irradiation, the
organically bound fission products must still be
separated from the precursor nuclides. But the
separation method to be used can instead be based on
physical rather than chemical properties of the
chemically bound nuclides. Specifically, gas
chromatography separations should be feasible. This
type of separation method has a number of
particularly important advantages. Consider the case
where it is of interest to examine specific fission
bromine nuclides. In this case the hot atom reactions
which would occur in organic liquids would probably
be experimentally favorable in that a larger
percentage of the bromine nuclides would form.
organic compounds than would selenium nuclides.
Further, any bromine formed from selenium beta-decay
would react to form organically-bound bromides. To
inhibit these latter reactions it is desirable to
separate the organic bromides from the selenium as
quickly as possible. Described in this thesis is a
semi-automatic injection method devised to permit
separation of the inorganic and organic fractions
within a few seconds after the end of the neutron
irradiation. The organic fraction is then carried by
helium flow gas to the gas chromatography column
where separation of the organic compounds begins to
occur. This latter separation procedure permits the
isolation of individual chemical compounds containing
in each compound all fission nuclides of the element
characterizing the compound.
